critique.summary: The article discusses a new German law, Energieeffizienzgesetz, effective July 1, 2026, which mandates that new data centers must reuse a minimum percentage of their waste heat, increasing from 10% in 2026 to 20% by 2028, with fines for non-compliance. The article implies this regulation is unprecedented among major data center markets and highlights the industry's previous underestimation of the law's impact.
mainPoints: Germany's Energieeffizienzgesetz requires new data centers from July 1, 2026, to reuse at least 10% of waste heat.
The reuse threshold increases to 15% in 2027 and 20% in 2028.
Non-compliance results in fines starting at EUR 50,000, increasing up to EUR 100,000.
The law was passed in September 2023 and took effect in November 2023.
The data center industry had largely underestimated the law's impact until the deadline approached.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately reports the existence and key provisions of Germany's Energieeffizienzgesetz, which mandates waste heat reuse in new data centers with escalating targets and penalties. This law is confirmed by official German legislative sources and industry reports. The claim that no other major data center market has implemented similar large-scale mandates aligns with current global regulatory landscapes. However, the article does not cite multiple sources or perspectives, limiting comprehensive verification.
considerations: The article does not discuss how data center operators plan to meet these reuse targets or the technological and economic challenges involved.
There is no mention of potential impacts on data center costs, design, or energy efficiency beyond heat reuse.
The article does not explore whether similar regulations might emerge in other countries or regions.
The preview nature of the article means full analysis and supporting evidence are not available for review.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the impact of 3M's exit from the PFAS market, touching on legal, environmental, and technological consequences. Commenters debate the health risks of PFAS, supply chain effects on immersion cooling technology, and potential alternative solutions for data center cooling and computing infrastructure.

comments.author: embedding-shape
comments.text:
> 3M did not make this call because they found a better product. They made it because they were staring down over 4,000 lawsuits and a $12.5 billion settlement with more than 11,000 U.S. public water systems alleging PFAS contamination in drinking water. The settlement received final court approval in March 2024. Payments stretch over 13 years.

> Who Got Hurt, Who Didn't

It seems to have had an massive impact, and article also includes a "who got hurt" but there is zero numbers about the number of people actually impacted by this catastrophe? I'm guessing the blogs focus might be on businesses, but considering this might be spawning something of a health crisis in the affected areas, maybe at least a mention of the humans involved here would make sense.
comments.analysis: This comment provides accurate legal context about 3M's PFAS liabilities and highlights a valid critique about the article's omission of human impact data.

comments.author: idbehold
comments.text: I guess the first ten upvotes are people who just read the headline as the link is currently incorrect and doesn't actually show the article with this headline. Link should be update to https://thecoolingreport.com/intel/pfas-two-phase-immersion-...
comments.analysis: The comment is factually correct about the broken link and helps improve access to the source material, aiding further discussion.

comments.author: api
comments.text:
In cold climate markets with a lot of fiber or other very fast access like very good 5G, why don’t AI companies or their cloud vendors market home or building scale heating compute nodes?

They could be built so that they exhaust waste heat into the HVAC system in winter and then switch to an outside piped radiator in the summer or something similar.

End users wouldn’t buy it. They’d make some kind of deal where one is installed and they pay less for heat and the extra electricity is paid by the compute operator via a separate meter. So the DC operator gets cooling that is (averaged over the year) almost free since they are basically reselling the heat for half the year or more.

For individual homes it might be unwieldy to manage a bunch of small units, so apartment/condo blocks and businesses might make more sense for installation. They could be colocated with building HVAC.

I guess economics depends on what percentage of DC cost is power or water for cooling.
comments.analysis: This is a creative and plausible proposal for improving energy efficiency in data centers, showing understanding of both technology and economics.

comments.author: mark-r
comments.text: I've never understood one vital thing - if PFAS is by nature totally inert and unreactive, how is it harmful?  If you drank a glass of the stuff, what would happen?
comments.analysis: The comment raises a reasonable question about PFAS toxicity, though it oversimplifies PFAS chemistry and ignores bioaccumulation and toxicity mechanisms.

comments.author: nubinetwork
comments.text: If we can't buy florinert anymore, does that mean we should go back to mineral oil?
comments.analysis: This comment correctly identifies a potential supply chain impact on cooling fluids and suggests a practical alternative.

comments.author: jokoon
comments.text:
I got a thin raincoat, a thing I can roll and pack in my bag, I have been carrying it around for the last 10 years or so.

I think it was advertised as very water proof, like water would pearl on it.

It's probably full of PFAS, no idea if it has been leeching PFAS, but I know it's not very waterproof anymore, so that might be a worrying clue.
comments.analysis: The anecdote highlights consumer exposure to PFAS but lacks scientific evidence about leaching or health impact.

comments.author: amelius
comments.text: Big fan of less DCs and more local computing. Hopefully this will help restore the RAM market.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses a personal opinion linking data center trends to hardware markets, but lacks detailed reasoning or evidence.
